title: CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies
abstract: 开环模仿学习推动了现代自动驾驶策略架构的进步，但闭环部署仍容易受到策略引起的分布偏移影响。现有后训练范式存在根本性权衡：闭环RL微调可提供来自执行动作的接地反馈，但受限于信息性事件的稀疏性；而反事实微调虽能对候选未来提供密集监督，但继承了不完美未来估计带来的偏差。本文提出反事实到交互式强化微调（Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning，CRAFT），这是一种在线策略框架，将闭环后训练建模为代理残差优化。CRAFT使用组归一化反事实优势作为真实闭环优势的密集代理，并通过来自交互关键事件的接地残差修正，将该代理与闭环世界对齐。为稳定适应过程，CRAFT通过不对称KL自蒸馏将在线策略正则化至EMA教师模型。理论上，CRAFT在相同访问状态分布下将真实闭环策略梯度分解为代理项和残差项，通过对齐代理降低残差方差，同时通过接地残差近似缓解代理偏差。实验方面，CRAFT在Bench2Drive数据集上，在分层规划、视觉-语言-动作和词汇评分架构中均取得最强闭环性能增益。消融实验、扩展行为、稳定性分析及迁移结果进一步验证了密集反事实代理与接地残差修正的互补作用。项目主页：https://currychen77.github.io/CRAFT。

CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies
Keyu Chen
1
Nanfei Ye
2

2

2
Li Auto Inc
Abstract
Open-loop imitation learning has advanced modern autonomous driving policy architectures, but closed-loop deployment remains vulnerable to policy-induced distribution shift. Existing post-training paradigms exhibit fundamental trade-offs: closed-loop RL fine-tuning provides grounded feedback from executed actions but is constrained by the sparsity of informative events, whereas counterfactual fine-tuning provides dense supervision over candidate futures but inherits bias from imperfect future estimates. We introduce
Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT)
, an on-policy framework that formulates closed-loop post-training as proxy-residual optimization. CRAFT uses group-normalized counterfactual advantages as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages and aligns this proxy with the closed-loop world through grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events. To stabilize adaptation, CRAFT regularizes the online policy toward an EMA teacher via asymmetric KL self-distillation. Theoretically, CRAFT decomposes the real closed-loop policy gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution, reducing residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation. Empirically, CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures. Ablations, scaling behavior, stability analyses, and transfer results further validate the complementary roles of dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction. Project page:
https://currychen77.github.io/CRAFT
.
1
Introduction
Modern autonomous driving policies have rapidly evolved beyond modular perception–planning pipelines. Representative frameworks include unified end-to-end models
[
13
,
17
,
38
]
, vision-language-action models
[
30
,
3
]
, and world action models
[
41
,
19
]
. While these architectures expand the capability of driving policies, their training remains largely governed by an open-loop imitation learning (IL) paradigm. This paradigm often leads to distribution shift due to the inherent discrepancy between open-loop training and closed-loop deployment. Therefore, further improving the closed-loop performance of pre-trained driving policies remains critical for safe and reliable deployment.
To address the challenges of distribution shift, two distinct post-training paradigms have emerged. As illustrated in
Figure
˜
1
, closed-loop RL fine-tunes pre-trained driving policies within interactive simulators to obtain grounded feedback from executed actions. However, the inherent sparsity of informative events across diverse scenarios often constrains the training efficiency of pure closed-loop RL. Conversely, counterfactual fine-tuning offers an alternative by evaluating multiple candidate futures from the same visited state, transforming these relative performance differences into dense supervisory signals through group-relative objectives, such as GRPO
[
35
]
. Nevertheless, counterfactual estimates of the future remain inherently biased, as the counterfactual world cannot faithfully capture the interaction dynamics of the real closed-loop world.
Figure 1:
Two existing post-training paradigms exhibit complementary trade-offs. Closed-loop RL fine-tuning provides grounded but sparse feedback from executed actions, whereas counterfactual fine-tuning provides dense supervision from biased future estimates. CRAFT combines their complementary strengths through a dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction.
The complementary strengths of these two paradigms motivate a natural question: can counterfactual supervision serve as a dense proxy, while closed-loop interaction corrects its residual mismatch with the real closed-loop world? CRAFT addresses this question by assigning distinct optimization roles to the two signals. Counterfactual supervision serves as a dense proxy over states visited by the current policy, whereas closed-loop interaction provides grounded residual correction to align this proxy with the real closed-loop world.
Building on this proxy-residual decomposition, we introduce Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), an on-policy post-training framework for pre-trained driving policies. As illustrated in
Figure
˜
1
, CRAFT uses the counterfactual world to evaluate groups of candidates from states visited by the online policy and converts their efficiency, safety, and rule-compliance returns into group-normalized counterfactual advantages. These advantages serve as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages. CRAFT then derives grounded residual correction from interaction-critical traffic events observed in executed closed-loop rollouts, aligning the proxy with the real closed-loop world through a value-free dual-clipped update
[
46
,
10
]
. To stabilize adaptation, asymmetric-KL self-distillation regularizes the online policy toward an EMA teacher initialized from the pre-trained policy, preserving reliable behaviors while allowing interaction-driven correction.
Theoretically, CRAFT decomposes the real closed-loop policy gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same state distribution, reducing residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation. Dual clipping further keeps rare critical events informative without dominating the update. Empirically, CRAFT achieves the largest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across policy architectures, including hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring. Ablations verify the complementary roles of each component, with the counterfactual proxy providing broad optimization signals, grounded residual correction delivering substantial closed-loop gains on interaction-sensitive metrics, and self-distillation stabilizing adaptation around reliable pre-trained behavior. Scaling and stability analyses further demonstrate that, with increasing fine-tuning data, CRAFT delivers more consistent gains and better training stability than baselines, while transfer results indicate partial robustness beyond the training distribution. Our main contributions are summarized as follows:
•
We formulate closed-loop post-training for pre-trained driving policies through a proxy-residual gradient decomposition, revealing the trade-off between dense but biased counterfactual supervision and sparse but grounded closed-loop interaction under the same visited-state distribution.
•
We propose CRAFT, an on-policy post-training framework that turns counterfactual supervision into a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages and uses interaction-critical closed-loop events as grounded residual correction, stabilized by self-distillation from the pre-trained policy.
•
We substantiate the proxy-residual design theoretically and empirically. CRAFT reduces residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation, and achieves consistent closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across diverse policy architectures, including hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring.
2
Related Work
End-to-End Autonomous Driving Policy.
Progress in end-to-end driving has been driven largely by advances in model architecture. Diffusion-based models
[
24
,
27
,
48
,

]
, vision-language-action models
[
6

32

49
,
40
]
, and trajectory-vocabulary scoring methods
[
21
,
44
,
22
,
37
]
all improve policy expressiveness. Yet their training remains largely imitation-based, with policies trained near expert states and then expected to remain reliable after their own actions shift future observations. This assumption is especially fragile in closed-loop deployment, where small planning errors can move the ego vehicle outside the training distribution.
Closed-Loop RL in Autonomous Driving.
Closed-loop RL mitigates distribution shift by optimizing policies on executed rollouts, often with policy-gradient methods such as PPO
[
33
]
in CARLA
[
7
,
15
]
or reconstructed simulators
[
8
,
9
,
29
]
. While grounded in real interaction, these methods remain sample-inefficient because informative events are rare, requiring extensive rollouts to obtain useful gradient estimates. Recent approaches improve efficiency through VLM-based reward densification
[
14
]
or world-model rollouts
[
18
,
43
]
, but their supervision depends on evaluator or model fidelity. CRAFT instead uses closed-loop interaction only to correct a dense counterfactual proxy, preserving grounded feedback without estimating full advantages from sparse rollouts.
Counterfactual Post-Training in Autonomous Driving.
Counterfactual post-training improves learning efficiency through dense local comparisons on real visited states
[
26
]
, making preference-based and group-relative objectives attractive
[
35
,
31
]
. Recent methods combine PDM Score
[
20
,
50
,
45
,
34
,
23
,
1
]
with GRPO in NAVSIM
[
4
]
for policy fine-tuning, or use synthetic counterfactual data
[
25
,
42
]
for offline post-training. A key limitation lies in the rollout condition, since counterfactual futures generated offline or in a non-reactive simulator do not capture true closed-loop interaction with the environment. CRAFT uses counterfactual futures as a dense proxy on real visited states, while grounded residual correction refines the proxy with feedback from true closed-loop rollouts.
3
Methodology
CRAFT is a general paradigm for closed-loop post-training of supervised driving policies. The target is to improve closed-loop performance under the on-policy state distribution. Existing post-training paradigms present a fundamental trade-off: closed-loop RL is physically grounded but relies on sparse, high-variance interaction feedback, whereas counterfactual optimization provides dense supervision but suffers from biased future estimates. CRAFT addresses this trade-off with two complementary components: a dense counterfactual proxy that provides trajectory-level supervision via candidate evaluation, and a grounded residual correction that compensates for proxy bias using executed closed-loop feedback. To preserve pre-trained behavior during fine-tuning, CRAFT further applies on-policy self-distillation with an EMA teacher. The framework is illustrated in
Figure
˜
2
.
Figure 2:
CRAFT combines dense counterfactual proxy supervision, grounded residual correction from real closed-loop interaction, and on-policy self-distillation toward reliable pre-trained behavior.

4
Experiments
4.1
Experimental Setup
Benchmark
We conduct experiments on Bench2Drive
[
16
]
, a CARLA-based benchmark
[
5
]
for closed-loop evaluation in autonomous driving. Our setup follows Bench2Drive as the primary protocol, with additional scenario configurations from LEAD
[
28
]
and Longest6 V2
[
15
]
. We also apply several protocol-level refinements to the original scenarios and evaluation protocol to improve efficiency and reduce repeated-run variance. Details are provided in
Appendix
˜
B
.
Metrics
We adopt the standard Bench2Drive metrics, including Driving Score (DS), Success Rate (SR), and Multi-Ability. DS combines Route Completion (RC) and Infraction Score (IS), where RC measures route progress and IS reflects safety and traffic-rule compliance. SR measures successful scenario completion, while Multi-Ability separately evaluates five advanced urban driving skills.
Driving Policies and Fine-tuning Baselines
To demonstrate the generality of CRAFT, we select three representative driving policies from distinct paradigms.
•
Hierarchical planning.
HiP-AD
[
39
]
uses multi-granularity planning queries together with deformable attention to couple perception and planning within a unified decoder.
•
Vision-language-action.
MindDrive
[
7
]
uses a VLA architecture with separate decision and action experts, translating high-level semantic decisions into low-level trajectory generation.
•
Vocabulary scoring.
SparseDriveV2
[
37
]
uses sparse scene representation for end-to-end driving, and here we use its vocabulary variant to provide a dense candidate-trajectory space for RL. This property also makes it the default driving policy in our subsequent analysis experiments.
We compare CRAFT against the pre-trained policy and three RL fine-tuning baselines under the same setting and evaluation protocol. The baselines differ only in their training objective.
•
PPO
[
33
]
employs a clipped surrogate objective together with a learned value baseline, making it the standard actor–critic baseline for stable on-policy fine-tuning.
•
REINFORCE++
[
12
]
removes the learned critic and uses return-based updates normalized at the batch level, providing a simple critic-free baseline for on-policy fine-tuning.
•
GRPO
[
35
]
replaces a separate critic with relative rewards computed within output groups, making it the closest baseline to the group-based counterfactual update used in CRAFT.
Implementation Details
For all policies, we fine-tune only the planning-related modules while freezing pre-trained visual processing. All fine-tuning methods share a CaRL-style reward formulation based on progress and infractions
[
15
]
. Our RL infrastructure is built on RIFT
[
2
]
and supports asynchronous rollout collection and policy updates. Details appear in
Sections
˜
D.2
,
D.3
,
D.4
and
D.5
.

4.3
Ablation and Generalization Analysis
The ablation study in
Table
˜
2
clarifies each component of CRAFT. Self-distillation reinforces the dominant modes of the pre-trained policy, while the dense counterfactual proxy drives broad closed-loop gains. With grounded residual correction, the full CRAFT objective achieves the best overall performance, especially on the interaction-related metrics IS and SR. These results support the proxy-residual view, where the dense counterfactual proxy provides the primary optimization signal and grounded residual correction compensates for counterfactual bias through closed-loop interaction.
As shown in
Table
˜
3
, CRAFT maintains positive generalization under scenario shift. In-domain fine-tuning on Bench2Drive delivers the largest improvement over the pre-trained policy, while cross-scenario transfer still yields consistent gains. The main limitation appears in Route Completion, where the limited improvement under transfer suggests a more conservative policy in unseen scenarios. Overall, CRAFT generalizes beyond the training split, although the magnitude of improvement remains partly dependent on the interaction distribution used for fine-tuning.
4.4
Scaling and Training Dynamics
Figure
˜
5
reveals a clear scaling pattern. CRAFT is the only method whose performance improves consistently with more data. PPO and REINFORCE++, which rely only on real interaction, become less stable at larger scales and show saturating gains, suggesting inefficient learning from sparse closed-loop feedback. GRPO, which depends only on proxy supervision, delivers limited improvement and even deteriorates at larger scales, indicating that proxy bias may become the main bottleneck in later stages of training. These trends demonstrate that efficient scaling requires dense proxy supervision, while sustained gains depend on grounded residual correction.
The results in
Figure
˜
5
further confirm the stability of CRAFT. In terms of KL stability, CRAFT shows larger gains for policies with broader action spaces, such as HiP-AD and SparseDriveV2, while the advantage is smaller for MindDrive, which has a more constrained action space. This pattern suggests that a broader action space improves counterfactual proxy stability. The expected-advantage results support the same conclusion from the grounded side. Compared with GRPO, CRAFT reduces expected-advantage variation across policy architectures. Although grounded residual correction is driven by sparse feedback, dual clipping stabilizes training. Together, these results suggest that the stability of CRAFT arises from both components, with broader action spaces improving proxy stability and dual-clipped grounded residual correction further reducing update variance.
4.5
Qualitative Scenario Analysis
Figure 6:
Policy distribution shift in a pedestrian-crossing scenario.
Top: a closed-loop scene snapshot with SparseDriveV2 as the ego vehicle. Bottom: the policy distributions over the trajectory vocabulary for the pre-trained and fine-tuned policies. CRAFT shifts probability mass toward braking-compatible modes.
Figure
˜
6
illustrates how fine-tuning reshapes the policy distribution in a safety-critical state. The pre-trained SparseDriveV2 assigns most probability mass to a left-turn accelerating mode, which remains largely unchanged after PPO and REINFORCE++ fine-tuning. This similarity suggests that sparse closed-loop updates do not efficiently shift the dominant mode away from unsafe behavior. GRPO shifts part of the distribution toward a braking-compatible mode, but this mode remains secondary, indicating that the counterfactual proxy alone gives a biased estimate of the safer closed-loop response. CRAFT induces the clearest shift, making the braking-compatible mode dominant while retaining the original peak of the pre-trained policy at lower probability. These results highlight the advantage of proxy-residual fine-tuning for safety-critical events by shifting probability toward interaction-grounded corrective behavior while preserving the pre-trained policy manifold.
5
Conclusions
This paper presents CRAFT, a proxy-residual framework for closed-loop post-training of supervised driving policies. CRAFT combines a dense counterfactual proxy on on-policy visited states, grounded residual correction from executed rollouts, and self-distillation to preserve reliable pre-trained behavior during fine-tuning. We support this design with an exact gradient decomposition on the real closed-loop state distribution, together with variance, bias, and stability analyses. On Bench2Drive, CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains across hierarchical-planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures, while ablation and scaling results confirm the complementary roles of counterfactual proxy learning, grounded residual correction, and self-distillation.

Appendix B
Bench2Drive Protocol
B.1
Motivation for Adjustments
Closed-loop training imposes fundamentally different requirements on a benchmark than closed-loop evaluation. Beyond accurate assessment, training requires clear and informative failure signals, as well as visually and physically consistent environment dynamics across policy updates to avoid non-stationarity, together with high rollout efficiency. In particular, rollouts should terminate immediately upon meaningful failure events to provide concise supervision, and unnecessary criteria overhead should be minimized, as rollout time directly dictates training efficiency.
We therefore adopt a patched protocol, Bench2Drive
∗
, which preserves the route set, ego-policy interface, and core metrics, while mitigating scenario artifacts that introduce non-policy noise into both training and evaluation.
B.2
Protocol-Level Adjustments
We apply six local adjustments to the official protocol.
•
Actor cleanup
. The official scenario manager can remove background or scenario-related actors at runtime, causing vehicles to disappear from the visual scene. We now clean up only background vehicles that block normal ego progress. Other nonblocking vehicles are kept in autopilot mode, and actors that would be removed immediately after spawning are skipped before they are spawned.
•
Timeout handling
. During both data collection and evaluation, we lower the upper bounds for scenario and route timeouts. This prevents a deadlocked route from consuming long simulator time after the policy has already produced the relevant failure signal.
•
BicycleFlow spawning
. We fix the BicycleFlow scenario so that cyclists are spawned only within the range specified by the scenario definition. The resulting flow follows normal behavior and avoids abnormal cyclist motion or collisions that are unrelated to the ego policy.
•
Route 3749 actor flow
. For route id 3749, we set the actor-flow speed to 12 m/s. This avoids an overly aggressive flow for the turning geometry, where the original setting could produce an invalid scene before the ego policy makes a meaningful decision.
•
Collision termination
. In both data collection and evaluation, a collision terminates the scene immediately. The original protocol may continue simulation after a collision; in BicycleFlow, for example, a cyclist can keep moving and generate repeated contacts from the same physical event. Early termination makes the failure state unambiguous and reduces noise in Driving Score. It may slightly lower Driving Score by removing post-collision driving segments, but it does not change Success Rate. We also ignore collision events with relative speed below 0.1 m/s to avoid duplicate contact records.
•
Town07 stop sign
. We ignore the invisible stop sign located at
(
0
,
0
,
0
)
(0,0,0)
in Town07. This removes spurious stop-sign violations caused by a map artifact rather than by policy behavior.

B.3
Sanity Check Against Official Bench2Drive
The protocol adjustments aim to produce cleaner RL rollouts without altering the benchmark’s qualitative semantics. We therefore compare the official protocol with Bench2Drive
∗
across five representative policies.
•
PDM-Lite
[
36
]
is a rule-based CARLA planner. It provides a nonlearned reference point whose behavior is driven by hand-designed planning logic rather than end-to-end perception and control.
•
PlanT v2
[
11
]
is a privileged planner with access to structured scene information. We include it to test whether the patched protocol remains consistent for high-performing planners that operate outside the sensor-only setting.
•
UniAD
[
13
]
is a planning-oriented end-to-end driving system. We use it as a representative learned policy with integrated perception, prediction, and planning modules.
•
VAD
[
17
]
is an end-to-end method based on vectorized scene representations. It offers a learned baseline with a different intermediate representation from UniAD while staying in the same sensor-driven evaluation regime.
•
SparseDrive
[
38
]
is an end-to-end method built around sparse scene representations. It is included because its closed-loop scores are close to the regime studied in this paper and because CRAFT is evaluated on SparseDrive-style policies.
Together, these policies cover rule-based, privileged, and end-to-end settings, allowing us to check whether the patched protocol changes efficiency and rollout cleanliness without altering the broad ordering of representative methods.
Table
˜
4
shows the expected pattern. The adjusted protocol is slightly stricter for strong planners, mainly because terminal events are handled more directly and avoidable post-failure rollout time is removed. For end-to-end methods, the ranking remains broadly stable. UniAD remains ahead of VAD and SparseDrive. Moreover, Bench2Drive
∗
produces substantially lower standard deviations than the official protocol, suggesting a more stable evaluation of closed-loop performance. This lower variance supports single-seed evaluation in the main experiments as a practical and cost-effective protocol for comparing relative method improvements under identical evaluation conditions. In practice, the shorter timeout and cleaner termination rules yield over 2
×
\times
higher closed-loop training throughput and over 3
×
\times
higher evaluation throughput. These efficiency gains are critical for reinforcement fine-tuning, where closed-loop rollout time is the primary bottleneck.

At each closed-loop state, the policy scores a finite set of candidate trajectories, and only the selected trajectory is passed to the controller to produce the action executed in the real environment. The counterfactual world later evaluates the stored candidate set from the same visited state, where counterfactual returns provide group-normalized counterfactual advantages without executing alternative candidates in CARLA. In parallel, the corrective reward is computed from the executed controller action and the resulting next state, then discounted, scaled, and clipped into the corrective advantage. Appendix D
Implementation Details
This section records the implementation choices used in the experiments in
Section
˜
4
. The main paper keeps the method description abstract; here we specify which policy parameters are updated, how candidate futures are simulated, and how the reward and optimization terms are instantiated.
D.1
Compute Resources
Our experiments are conducted on a single server equipped with eight NVIDIA RTX 4090D GPUs. CRAFT uses an asynchronous collect-and-train pipeline, where rollout workers interact with CARLA and write synchronized rollout snapshots to a shared buffer. The trainer then waits until enough rollout files are ready and aggregates them into a rollout batch, preprocesses the counterfactual and corrective signals, and performs one Lightning
1
1
1
https://github.com/Lightning-AI/pytorch-lightning
training iteration before releasing the collectors to continue with the updated policy version. Each driving policy uses four GPUs for closed-loop rollout collection and four GPUs for policy training; under this configuration, one complete fine-tuning run takes 6–10 hours, depending on the policy’s inference efficiency and the trainable modules. This design overlaps closed-loop data collection with policy optimization, which reduces idle time in the main training loop.
D.2
Trainable Modules of Driving Policies
We fine-tune only the modules that score candidate trajectories. All perception, map construction, detection, motion prediction, and trajectory-generation modules remain frozen. This restriction keeps sparse online rewards close to the policy decision boundary. The update can change which trajectory candidate is preferred, but it does not rewrite the visual or geometric driving stack.
Figure
˜
7
illustrates the operational paradigms of these driving policies.
•
HiP-AD
[
39
]
.
The ego decision is represented as joint path-speed candidates with 48 ego modes. CRAFT and the non-PPO baselines update only the final classification branch in the last plan-refinement layer. When PPO is used, a lightweight critic is trained in addition to this branch.
•
MindDrive
[
7
]
.
The high-level speed decision is chosen by a VLM decision expert before the policy generates the low-level path. Fine-tuning updates only the LoRA decision expert used for this high-level decision. The visual encoder, map and detection heads, language backbone, and action expert stay fixed. Under PPO, the critic is the only additional trainable component.
•
SparseDriveV2
†
[
37
]
.
The policy scores a 512-mode trajectory vocabulary. Fine-tuning updates only the final trajectory classification branch that assigns probabilities to vocabulary candidates. The trajectory vocabulary, temporal queue, perception backbone, map head, detection head, and trajectory decoder remain frozen. PPO adds the critic, while all other fine-tuning methods update only the classification branch.

D.3
Counterfactual World
The counterfactual world evaluates the policy candidate set at states visited by real closed-loop rollouts. For each observation, the Bench2Drive agent stores an auxiliary record containing candidate trajectories

By default, each candidate is simulated with a batched trajectory tracker. The tracker prepends the current ego pose to each candidate trajectory, transforms the local trajectory into the global CARLA frame, and flattens the batch and candidate dimensions into independent tracking problems. At each virtual step, a PID controller tracks a short reference segment, and a kinematic bicycle model advances the ego state. Nearby agents use a decay rollout model in which their current actions are held for a short decision window. The model then reduces throttle and ramps up braking, with a shorter ramp when an agent is on a lane connector. Collision checks use rectangular vehicle footprints and batched separating-axis tests.

preview_text:
Open-loop imitation learning has advanced modern autonomous driving policy architectures, but closed-loop deployment remains vulnerable to policy-induced distribution shift. Existing post-training paradigms exhibit fundamental trade-offs: closed-loop RL fine-tuning provides grounded feedback from executed actions but is constrained by the sparsity of informative events, whereas counterfactual fine-tuning provides dense supervision over candidate futures but inherits bias from imperfect future estimates. We introduce Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), an on-policy framework that formulates closed-loop post-training as proxy-residual optimization. CRAFT uses group-normalized counterfactual advantages as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages and aligns this proxy with the closed-loop world through grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events. To stabilize adaptation, CRAFT regularizes the online policy toward an EMA teacher via asymmetric KL self-distillation. Theoretically, CRAFT decomposes the real closed-loop policy gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution, reducing residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation. Empirically, CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures. Ablations, scaling behavior, stability analyses, and transfer results further validate the complementary roles of dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction. Project page: https://currychen77.github.io/CRAFT.

one_line_summary: 提出CRAFT框架，结合反事实与交互式强化学习微调，用于自动驾驶策略的闭环部署优化。
detailed_summary:
## 论文详细摘要

### 1. 研究背景和动机
当前自动驾驶策略多采用**开环模仿学习**进行预训练，但在闭环部署中易出现**策略诱导的分布偏移**问题。现有后训练范式存在根本性权衡：**闭环强化学习**提供基于真实交互的反馈，但有效事件稀疏、训练效率低；**反事实微调**提供密集监督信号，但反事实世界无法准确模拟真实闭环交互，存在固有偏差。

### 2. 核心方法和技术创新
本文提出 **CRAFT（反事实到交互式强化微调）** 框架，将闭环后训练形式化为**代理-残差优化**问题：
- **密集反事实代理**：在在线策略访问的状态上，通过组归一化的反事实优势作为真实闭环优势的密集代理
- **有根据的残差校正**：从交互关键事件中提取有根据的残差信号，校正代理偏差
- **非对称KL自蒸馏**：通过EMA教师正则化在线策略，保持预训练策略的可靠行为

理论上，CRAFT将真实闭环策略梯度分解为代理项和残差项，在相同访问状态分布下，用对齐的代理降低残差方差，同时通过有根据的残差近似减轻代理偏差。

### 3. 主要实验结果
在**Bench2Drive**基准上，CRAFT在**层次规划、视觉-语言-动作、词汇评分**三种架构上均取得最强闭环性能提升。消融实验验证了各组件的互补作用：反事实代理提供广泛优化信号，有根据的残差校正带来交互敏感指标的显著提升，自蒸馏稳定了微调过程。缩放实验表明CRAFT随数据量增加持续改进，而基线方法出现饱和或退化。

### 4. 研究意义和价值
CRAFT为自动驾驶策略的闭环后训练提供了一种**通用、高效、稳定**的范式，通过巧妙结合反事实和交互信号的优势，显著提升了预训练策略的闭环性能，对自动驾驶安全部署具有重要实际价值。

qa_pairs.question: 这篇论文的核心创新点是什么，他想解决什么问题，怎么解决的？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 论文核心创新点分析

### 一、核心问题

论文旨在解决**自动驾驶策略在闭环部署中的分布偏移问题**。具体而言：

- **开环模仿学习**训练的模型在闭环部署时，由于训练与部署环境不一致，会产生策略诱导的分布偏移。
- 现有后训练范式存在**根本性权衡**：
  - **闭环强化学习**：提供真实的交互反馈，但信息性事件稀疏，训练效率低。
  - **反事实微调**：提供密集的监督信号，但反事实世界的估计存在偏差，无法真实反映闭环交互动态。

### 二、核心创新点

**CRAFT（Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning）** 的核心创新在于提出了 **“代理-残差”优化框架**，将闭环后训练形式化为代理-残差优化问题。

#### 1. 密集反事实代理（Dense Counterfactual Proxy）

- **作用**：在在线策略访问的状态上，利用反事实世界评估候选轨迹组，生成组归一化的反事实优势值，作为真实闭环优势的密集代理。
- **优势**：提供密集的、轨迹级别的监督信号，覆盖大多数访问状态，提高训练效率。

#### 2. 有根据的残差校正（Grounded Residual Correction）

- **作用**：从实际执行的闭环交互中提取关键事件（如碰撞、交通规则违反）的反馈，通过双裁剪更新校正反事实代理与真实闭环世界之间的残差。
- **优势**：提供真实的、交互关键的校正信号，弥补反事实代理的偏差。

#### 3. 非对称KL自蒸馏（Asymmetric KL Self-Distillation）

- **作用**：通过EMA教师网络对在线策略进行正则化，保留预训练策略的可靠行为，同时允许交互驱动的校正。
- **优势**：稳定微调过程，防止策略偏离预训练的可靠行为。

### 三、解决方案的技术细节

#### 1. 理论框架：代理-残差梯度分解

论文从理论上证明了真实闭环策略梯度可以分解为：

```
∇θJ_real(πθ) = E[∇θlogπθ(τ|s)Φπθ(s,τ)] + E[∇θlogπθ(τ|s)Δπθ(s,τ)]
```

- **代理项**：密集反事实优势，提供主要优化信号。
- **残差项**：反事实代理与真实优势之间的偏差，通过有根据的闭环反馈校正。

#### 2. 算法实现

```python
# CRAFT 总损失函数
ℒ_all = λ_cp * ℒ_cp + λ_gr * ℒ_gr + β_r * ℒ_dist + β_f * ℒ_KL
```

- **ℒ_cp**：反事实代理损失，基于组归一化的反事实优势。
- **ℒ_gr**：有根据的残差损失，使用双裁剪PPO更新。
- **ℒ_dist**：反向KL散度，约束策略接近教师网络。
- **ℒ_KL**：前向KL散度，防止策略坍缩。

#### 3. 关键技术创新点总结

| 创新点 | 解决的问题 | 实现方式 |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| 密集反事实代理 | 闭环RL的稀疏反馈问题 | 在在线状态上评估候选轨迹组，生成密集优势 |
| 有根据的残差校正 | 反事实微调的偏差问题 | 从实际交互中提取关键事件，校正代理偏差 |
| 非对称KL自蒸馏 | 微调过程中的策略漂移 | EMA教师网络正则化，保留可靠行为 |
| 双裁剪更新 | 稀有灾难性事件的稳定性 | 防止极端负事件主导更新 |

### 四、实际价值

1. **通用性**：在三种不同架构（层次规划、视觉-语言-动作、词汇评分）上均取得最佳闭环性能提升。
2. **可扩展性**：随着微调数据增加，CRAFT是唯一性能持续提升的方法。
3. **稳定性**：训练更稳定，KL散度和期望优势的变异系数更低。
4. **安全性**：在安全关键场景（如行人过街）中，能有效将策略分布从危险模式转向制动模式。

qa_pairs.question: 基于他的前作，梳理这个方向的整个发展脉络，每一步相比于之前的工作都改进了什么，着重于几个不同的发展方向。
qa_pairs.answer:
好的，作为专业的学术论文分析助手，我将基于您提供的论文《CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies》及其引用的相关工作，梳理自动驾驶策略后训练（Post-Training）领域的发展脉络。我将重点关注不同技术路线及其演进，并分析每一步相比之前工作的改进。

## 自动驾驶策略后训练技术发展脉络分析

本文的核心是解决自动驾驶策略在**开环模仿学习（Open-loop IL）** 训练后，在**闭环部署（Closed-loop Deployment）** 中面临的**分布偏移（Distribution Shift）** 问题。后训练（Post-Training）旨在弥合这一差距。该领域的发展主要围绕两个核心挑战：**学习信号的密度**和**反馈的准确性**。

### 1. 起点：开环模仿学习（Open-loop IL）

-   **方法**：在专家轨迹数据上训练策略，最小化预测轨迹与专家轨迹之间的差异。
-   **核心问题**：训练和部署环境不一致。策略在训练时看到的都是专家状态，但在部署时，其自身的微小误差会导致进入从未见过的状态（分布偏移），错误会迅速累积，导致灾难性后果。
-   **代表工作**：UniAD, VAD, SparseDrive 等早期端到端模型。

### 2. 发展方向一：闭环强化学习（Closed-loop RL Fine-tuning）

这是最直接的改进方向，旨在通过与环境真实交互来获得**准确**的反馈。

-   **核心思想**：在交互式模拟器中，让策略执行动作，并根据真实的奖励信号（如任务完成度、安全性）进行优化。
-   **改进**：从根本上解决了开环训练的问题，提供了**基于真实交互的、物理上准确的（grounded）** 反馈。
-   **核心瓶颈**：**信号稀疏性（Sparsity）**。在大多数驾驶场景中，关键事件（如碰撞、违规）发生频率极低。策略在大部分时间只能获得微弱的奖励信号，导致学习效率低下，需要海量的交互样本。
-   **代表工作**：
    -   **PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)**：标准的策略梯度方法，通过裁剪（clipping）保证更新稳定性。但受限于稀疏奖励，样本效率低。
    -   **REINFORCE++**：去掉了价值网络（critic），简化了流程，但同样受限于稀疏的蒙特卡洛回报。
    -   **CaRL**：通过设计更精细的奖励函数（如进度、路线遵守、安全）来尝试缓解稀疏性问题，但本质上仍依赖真实交互。

### 3. 发展方向二：反事实微调（Counterfactual Fine-tuning）

为了克服闭环RL的稀疏性问题，研究者开始探索利用**反事实推理**来生成更密集的学习信号。

-   **核心思想**：在同一个状态下，评估多个候选轨迹（反事实的未来），通过比较它们的相对优劣来生成密集的监督信号。
-   **改进**：极大地**增加了学习信号的密度**。在每个状态，策略都能从多个候选轨迹的对比中获得信息，而不是只依赖一个执行动作的结果。
-   **核心瓶颈**：**估计偏差（Bias）**。反事实世界是**非交互的（non-reactive）** 或基于简化模型的，无法精确模拟真实世界中其他智能体对自车动作的反应。因此，反事实的评估（如PDM Score）是对真实闭环优势的有偏估计。
-   **代表工作**：
    -   **GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization)**：在组内对候选轨迹的回报进行归一化，生成相对优势。这是CRAFT中密集代理（Dense Proxy）的直接前身。
    -   **基于PDM Score的方法**：使用非反应式模拟器（如NAVSIM）中的PDM Score作为反事实评估标准。

### 4. 发展方向三：CRAFT - 代理-残差优化（Proxy-Residual Optimization）

CRAFT 的核心洞察是，上述两个方向存在**互补的权衡**：闭环RL提供**准确但稀疏**的信号，反事实微调提供**密集但有偏**的信号。CRAFT 的创新在于将两者有机地结合起来，形成一个统一的框架。

-   **核心思想**：将真实的闭环策略梯度分解为两个部分：
    1.  **密集代理项（Dense Proxy Term）**：使用反事实优势（如GRPO的组归一化优势）作为真实优势的密集代理。
    2.  **有根据的残差校正项（Grounded Residual Correction Term）**：使用来自真实闭环交互的、关键事件的反馈（如碰撞、违规）来校正代理项与真实优势之间的残差。

-   **关键改进**：
    -   **解决了信号密度与准确性的矛盾**：通过代理项提供广泛的、密集的优化信号，通过残差项提供精确的、基于真实交互的校正。两者在同一状态分布下定义，避免了分布不匹配。
    -   **理论支撑**：论文从理论上证明了这种分解可以降低方差（当代理与真实优势足够对齐时）并控制偏差（通过有根据的残差近似）。
    -   **稳定训练**：引入了非对称KL自蒸馏（Asymmetric KL Self-Distillation）和双重裁剪（Dual Clipping）技术，确保策略在利用新信号的同时，不会偏离预训练策略的可靠行为，并防止罕见但极端的负事件主导更新。

-   **与之前工作的关系**：
    -   **优于纯闭环RL (PPO, REINFORCE++)**：通过密集代理项，CRAFT 的学习效率远高于仅依赖稀疏真实交互的方法。
    -   **优于纯反事实微调 (GRPO)**：通过有根据的残差校正，CRAFT 能够纠正反事实世界的偏差，在安全关键场景（如行人横穿、路口交互）中表现显著优于GRPO。
    -   **通用性**：CRAFT 被证明在多种不同的策略架构（分层规划、视觉-语言-动作、词汇评分）上均能取得一致的性能提升，表明其是一个通用的后训练范式。

### 总结：发展脉络图

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[开环模仿学习 (Open-loop IL)] --> B{核心问题: 分布偏移}；
    
    B --> C[发展方向一: 闭环RL微调]；
    C --> C1[代表: PPO, REINFORCE++]；
    C1 --> C2[优点: 反馈准确 (Grounded)]；
    C1 --> C3[缺点: 信号稀疏 (Sparse)]；

    B --> D[发展方向二: 反事实微调]；
    D --> D1[代表: GRPO]；
    D1 --> D2[优点: 信号密集 (Dense)]；
    D1 --> D3[缺点: 反馈有偏 (Biased)]；

    C & D --> E[发展方向三: CRAFT (代理-残差优化)]；
    E --> E1[核心: 结合两者优点]；
    E1 --> E2[密集代理项 (来自反事实)]；
    E1 --> E3[有根据的残差校正项 (来自闭环交互)]；
    E --> E4[优点: 信号密集 + 反馈准确]；
    E --> E5[稳定技术: 非对称KL蒸馏 + 双重裁剪]；
```

**结论**：CRAFT 并非一个孤立的创新，而是对前两个发展方向（闭环RL和反事实微调）的深刻理解和有机融合。它通过“代理-残差”分解，巧妙地解决了“信号密度”与“信号准确性”这一对核心矛盾，代表了自动驾驶策略后训练领域一个重要的范式进步。

qa_pairs.question: 他的前作有哪些？使用表格仔细讲讲他的每篇前作，他和前作的区别是什么，主要改善是什么？着重于具体相比于之前文章的改动
qa_pairs.answer:
好的，根据您提供的论文《CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies》，我将分析其前作，并详细对比CRAFT与这些前作的区别和主要改进。 ## 前作分析 根据论文内容，CRAFT的主要前作可以归纳为两类：**纯闭环强化学习（Closed-loop RL）** 和 **纯反事实微调（Counterfactual Fine-tuning）**。论文中引用了多个具体方法作为代表，下面我将以表格形式详细分析。 ### 前作对比表格 | 前作类别 | 代表方法 | 核心思想 | 与CRAFT的区别 | CRAFT的主要改进 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **纯闭环强化学习** | **PPO** (Proximal Policy Optimization) [33] | 在交互式模拟器中执行动作，从真实的闭环反馈（奖励）中学习。使用价值网络（Critic）来估计优势函数，并通过裁剪（clipping）来稳定更新。 | 1. **信号稀疏性**: PPO的反馈信号来自真实交互，但关键事件（如碰撞）稀疏，导致训练效率低。
2. **高方差**: 由于稀疏性，价值估计和梯度更新方差大，不稳定。 | 1. **引入密集代理信号**: CRAFT使用反事实世界提供密集的、组归一化的优势作为代理信号，弥补了闭环RL信号稀疏的缺点。
2. **代理-残差分解**: CRAFT将闭环梯度分解为密集代理项和接地残差项，前者提供广泛优化信号，后者用真实交互修正代理偏差。 | | **纯闭环强化学习** | **REINFORCE++** [12] | 同样是闭环RL，但去除了价值网络（Critic-free），使用批归一化的蒙特卡洛回报作为优势函数。 | 1. **信号稀疏性**: 与PPO相同，依赖稀疏的真实交互信号。
2. **无价值函数**: 虽然简化了架构，但无法利用价值函数进行方差缩减，更新方差可能更大。 | 1. **引入密集代理信号**: 同PPO对比，CRAFT的密集代理信号提供了更稳定的学习信号。
2. **接地残差修正**: CRAFT的接地残差项（来自真实交互）专注于修正代理偏差，而不是像REINFORCE++那样依赖整个稀疏的回报。 | | **纯反事实微调** | **GRPO** (Group Relative Policy Optimization) [35] | 在同一个访问过的状态下，评估一组候选轨迹，并计算组内相对优势（组归一化），以此作为密集的监督信号。不依赖真实闭环交互。 | 1. **代理偏差**: 反事实世界无法完美模拟真实闭环交互的动态，其评估的未来存在固有偏差。
2. **无接地修正**: 完全依赖有偏差的代理信号，无法通过真实交互来纠正错误。 | 1. **引入接地残差修正**: CRAFT在密集代理信号的基础上，增加了来自真实闭环交互的接地残差修正项，专门用于修正代理偏差。
2. **代理-残差分解**: 明确将优化目标分解为代理和残差两部分，并证明这种分解可以降低方差并修正偏差。 | ### 主要改善总结 CRAFT的核心改善在于它**创造性地结合了两种前作范式的互补优势**，并提出了一个理论化的框架来指导这种结合。 1. **从“二选一”到“互补结合”**: - **前作**: 要么选择**稀疏但真实**的闭环RL信号（PPO, REINFORCE++），要么选择**密集但有偏差**的反事实信号（GRPO）。 - **CRAFT**: 提出**代理-残差分解**，将两者结合。反事实信号作为**密集代理**提供广泛优化方向，而闭环交互信号作为**接地残差**来修正代理的偏差。 2. **理论上的严谨性**: - **前作**: 多为经验性方法，缺乏对两种信号如何最优结合的深入理论分析。 - **CRAFT**: 提供了**精确的梯度分解**（Proposition 3.1），证明了在相同状态分布下，闭环梯度可以分解为代理项和残差项。还提供了**方差缩减**（Proposition 3.2）和**梯度偏差**（Theorem 3.3）的理论分析，为设计提供了理论支撑。 3. **具体的技术改进**: - **双裁剪（Dual Clipping）**: 在处理接地残差项时，CRAFT采用了双裁剪技术，确保罕见的灾难性事件（如碰撞）能提供信息丰富的梯度，但又不会主导整个更新，从而稳定训练。 - **非对称KL自蒸馏（Asymmetric KL Self-Distillation）**: 为了在微调过程中保持预训练策略的可靠行为，CRAFT使用了EMA教师模型和非对称KL散度（反向KL为主，前向KL为辅）进行约束，防止策略发生剧烈偏移。这比简单的KL正则化更精细。 4. **实证上的通用性**: - **前作**: 通常在特定架构上验证。 - **CRAFT**: 在**分层规划（HiP-AD）、视觉-语言-动作（MindDrive）和词汇评分（SparseDriveV2）** 三种完全不同的主流驾驶策略架构上均取得了最显著的闭环性能提升，证明了其方法的通用性。 **总结来说，CRAFT不是简单地将前作方法拼凑，而是通过理论分析，设计了一个精巧的框架，让“密集但有偏”的反事实信号和“稀疏但真实”的闭环信号各司其职、协同工作，从而在自动驾驶策略的闭环后训练中取得了显著的性能提升。**

qa_pairs.question: 论文提出了哪些关键技术方法，请列表格具体详细说明技术细节，需要包含具体的数学原理推导，以及具体参数。
qa_pairs.answer:
好的，作为一名专业的学术论文分析助手，我将对论文《CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies》的核心技术方法进行详细分析。 ## 论文核心技术方法详解 CRAFT框架的核心思想是**代理-残差优化**，它巧妙地结合了反事实微调（密集但有偏）和闭环强化学习（稀疏但真实）的优势。下面我将通过表格形式，详细阐述其关键技术细节、数学原理和具体参数。 ### 核心技术方法总览 | 技术组件 | 核心思想 | 数学原理与推导 | 关键参数与实现细节 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1. 代理-残差梯度分解** | 将真实闭环策略梯度分解为密集的**反事实代理项**和基于真实交互的**残差校正项**，两者定义在相同的在线策略状态分布上。 | **公式 (3):**
$\nabla_{\theta}J_{\text{real}}(\pi_{\theta}) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta}\log\pi_{\theta}(\tau\|s) \Phi_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)] + \mathbb{E}[\nabla_{\theta}\log\pi_{\theta}(\tau\|s) \Delta_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)]$
其中 $\Delta_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau) = A^{\text{real}}_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau) - \Phi_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)$ 是残差。

**方差减少 (Proposition 3.2):**
当反事实代理 $\Phi$ 与真实优势 $A^{\text{real}}$ 足够相关时（即 $Cov[Y, C] > \frac{1}{2}Var[C]$），残差项 $\Delta$ 的方差小于真实优势 $A^{\text{real}}$ 的方差，从而降低整体梯度估计的方差。 | - **状态分布:** $d_{\text{real}}^{\pi_{\theta}}$ (在线策略的真实闭环状态分布)
- **代理值:** $\Phi_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)$ (反事实优势)
- **残差:** $\Delta_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)$ (真实优势与代理优势之差) | | **2. 密集反事实代理** | 在在线策略访问的状态下，利用一个**非反应式**的反事实世界模拟器，对一组候选轨迹进行快速评估，生成密集的、组内归一化的优势信号。 | **公式 (6) & (7):**
**组归一化优势:**
$\tilde{A}_{i,g} = \frac{\tilde{R}_{i,g} - \mu_i}{\sigma_i + \varepsilon_{\text{norm}}}$
其中 $\tilde{R}_{i,g}$ 是反事实世界中的累计回报，$\mu_i$ 和 $\sigma_i$ 是组内均值和标准差。

**反事实代理损失:**
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{cp}} = -\frac{1}{B}\sum_{i=1}^{B}\sum_{g=1}^{G}\pi_{\theta}(\tau_g\|s_i)\tilde{A}_{i,g}$
这是一个在离散动作空间上的精确期望。 | - **候选轨迹数量:** $G$ (每组)
- **反事实世界:** 基于运动学自行车模型和PID跟踪器的非反应式模拟器。
- **反事实回报 $\tilde{R}_{i,g}$:** 包含效率、安全、规则遵守等项，具体见附录D.4。
- **归一化参数:** $\varepsilon_{\text{norm}} = 10^{-8}$, $\sigma_{\min} = 5.0$ (标准差下限)
- **损失权重:** $\lambda_{\text{cp}} = 1.0$ | | **3. 有根据的残差校正** | 利用真实闭环交互中发生的**关键事件**（如碰撞、违规）产生的稀疏但真实的反馈，来校正反事实代理的偏差。 | **公式 (8) & (9):**
**重要性采样比率:** $\rho_t(\theta) = \pi_{\theta}(\tau_t\|s_t) / \pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}(\tau_t\|s_t)$

**双裁剪损失:**
$\ell_t(\theta) = \min(\rho_t(\theta)\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t, \text{clip}(\rho_t(\theta), 1-\epsilon_{\text{clip}}, 1+\epsilon_{\text{clip}})\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t)$
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{gr}} = -\mathbb{E}_{t\in\mathcal{B}}[\max(\ell_t(\theta), c\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t)\mathbf{1}_{\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t<0} + \ell_t(\theta)\mathbf{1}_{\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t\geq 0}]$

**梯度偏差界 (Theorem 3.3):**
$\|\hat{g}(\theta) - g(\theta)\|_2 \leq (\mathbb{E}\|\nabla_{\theta}\log\pi_{\theta}(\tau\|s)\|_2^2)^{1/2} (\mathbb{E}\|\hat{\Delta}_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau) - \Delta_{\pi_{\theta}}(s,\tau)\|^2)^{1/2}$
该定理表明，残差校正的偏差受限于残差近似误差。 | - **校正优势 $\hat{A}^{\text{gr}}_t$:** 从稀疏的**校正奖励** $r^{\text{gr}}_t$ 计算得到。
- **校正奖励 $r^{\text{gr}}_t$:** 仅对碰撞、违规等事件给予惩罚，具体见附录D.4。
- **PPO裁剪范围:** $\epsilon_{\text{clip}} = 0.2$
- **双裁剪常数:** $c = 2.0$ (用于防止极端负面事件主导更新)
- **校正折扣因子:** $\gamma_c = 0.8$
- **校正回报缩放:** $S_{\text{gr}} = 8.0$
- **校正优势裁剪范围:** $[A_{\min}, A_{\max}] = [-1, 1]$
- **损失权重:** $\lambda_{\text{gr}} = 0.5$ | | **4. 在线策略自蒸馏** | 通过一个EMA教师网络（初始化为预训练策略）对在线策略进行正则化，防止策略在微调过程中过度偏离可靠的预训练行为。 | **公式 (12) & (13):**
**EMA教师更新:**
$\theta_T^{(k+1)} \leftarrow m\theta_T^{(k)} + (1-m)\theta^{(k)}$

**非对称KL散度损失:**
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{dist}} = \mathbb{E}_{s\sim d_{\text{real}}^{\pi_{\theta}}, \tau\sim\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\|s)}[\log\frac{\pi_{\theta}(\tau\|s)}{\pi_T(\tau\|s)}]$ (反向KL，主要约束)
$\mathcal{L}_{\text{KL}} = \mathbb{E}_{s\sim d_{\text{real}}^{\pi_{\theta}}, \tau\sim\pi_T(\cdot\|s)}[\log\frac{\pi_T(\tau\|s)}{\pi_{\theta}(\tau\|s)}]$ (前向KL，辅助约束)

**理论依据 (Proposition A.1):**
在反向KL约束下，最优策略会依据代理-残差优势 $U(s,\tau)$ 对教师分布进行指数加权重排，实现有原则的策略提升。 | - **EMA动量:** $m = 0.99$
- **教师初始化:** $\theta_T^{(0)} = \theta_{\text{pre}}$ (预训练权重)
- **反向KL权重:** $\beta_r = 0.5$ (主要约束，防止策略漂移)
- **前向KL权重:** $\beta_f = 0.1$ (辅助约束，防止模式坍塌) | ### 总结 CRAFT的核心技术创新在于其**代理-残差优化框架**。它不像传统方法那样非此即彼，而是： 1. **理论驱动**：通过严格的梯度分解（公式3）和方差分析（Proposition 3.2），为结合两种互补信号提供了坚实的理论基础。 2. **实践有效**：通过**密集反事实代理**提供高效的优化信号，通过**有根据的残差校正**提供精准的纠偏，并通过**自蒸馏**保证训练的稳定性。 3. **参数明确**：论文提供了所有关键超参数（如 $\lambda_{\text{cp}}, \lambda_{\text{gr}}, \beta_r, \beta_f, \epsilon_{\text{clip}}, c$ 等），使得该方法具有高度的可复现性

qa_pairs.question: 他使用了哪些评价指标与数据集，列表格具体讲讲他的评价指标的细节与数据集的细节
qa_pairs.answer:
## 评价指标与数据集细节

### 数据集：Bench2Drive

- **基础平台**：基于 **CARLA** 模拟器的自动驾驶闭环评估基准
- **场景来源**：包含多种城市驾驶场景，如变道、超车、紧急制动、让行、交通标志等
- **协议调整**：论文采用了修正版协议 **Bench2Drive\***，主要改进包括：
  - 碰撞后立即终止场景，避免重复接触噪声
  - 调整超时上限，提高训练和评估效率
  - 修复自行车流生成、演员清理等场景工件
  - 修正 Town07 地图中不可见停车标志的误判
- **额外数据集**：还使用了 **LEAD** 和 **Longest6 V2** 场景配置进行泛化测试

### 评价指标细节

| 指标 | 全称 | 计算方式 | 意义 |
|------|------|----------|------|
| **DS** | Driving Score | **DS = RC × IS**，即路线完成率与违规分数的乘积 | 综合衡量路线完成质量和安全性，越高越好 |
| **RC** | Route Completion | 实际完成的路线进度百分比 | 反映策略能否有效完成预定路线 |
| **IS** | Infraction Score | 基于安全与交通规则遵守情况的分数（范围 0~1） | 反映策略的安全性，包括碰撞、违规等 |
| **SR** | Success Rate | 成功完成场景的比例（百分比） | 反映策略在完整场景中的成功率 |
| **Multi-Ability** | 多能力评估 | 分别评估五种高级驾驶技能：**Merging**（并道）、**Overtaking**（超车）、**Emergency Brake**（紧急制动）、**Give Way**（让行）、**Traffic Sign**（交通标志） | 细粒度评估策略在不同驾驶场景下的专项能力，取各能力百分比的平均值 |

### 评价指标使用方式

- 所有指标均在 **闭环（closed-loop）** 评估下计算
- 对于修正协议 Bench2Drive\*，结果报告为 **均值 ± 标准差**（3个随机种子）
- 主实验中采用 **单种子评估**，因为修正协议已显著降低方差
- 多能力指标在雷达图中可视化，便于对比不同方法在各专项能力上的表现

qa_pairs.question: 论文在哪些数据集上进行了实验？主要的评估指标和性能提升是多少？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 实验数据集、评估指标与性能提升

### 实验数据集

论文主要在以下数据集/基准上进行实验：

- **Bench2Drive**：基于CARLA的闭环评估基准，是主要实验协议
- **LEAD**：额外的场景配置来源
- **Longest6 V2**：额外的场景配置来源

### 主要评估指标

论文采用Bench2Drive标准指标：

1. **Driving Score (DS)**：驾驶得分，结合路线完成率（RC）和违规得分（IS）
2. **Success Rate (SR)**：成功完成场景的比率
3. **Multi-Ability**：多能力评估，包括五个高级驾驶技能：
   - Merging（并道）
   - Overtaking（超车）
   - Emergency Brake（紧急制动）
   - Give Way（让行）
   - Traffic Sign（交通标志）

### 性能提升（CRAFT vs 预训练模型）

| 驾驶策略 | 指标 | 预训练 | CRAFT | 提升 |
|---------|------|--------|-------|------|
| **HiP-AD** (分层规划) | DS | 73.46 | **75.79** | +2.33 |
| | SR (%) | 53.64 | **56.36** | +2.72 |
| | Multi-Ability Mean (%) | 51.80 | **55.20** | +3.40 |
| **MindDrive** (视觉-语言-动作) | DS | 62.60 | **67.45** | +4.85 |
| | SR (%) | 40.45 | **46.36** | +5.91 |
| | Multi-Ability Mean (%) | 40.00 | **46.60** | +6.60 |
| **SparseDriveV2†** (词汇评分) | DS | 66.43 | **71.10** | +4.67 |
| | SR (%) | 45.45 | **50.45** | +5.00 |
| | Multi-Ability Mean (%) | 44.60 | **49.90** | +5.30 |

### 关键发现

- CRAFT在所有三种不同架构的驾驶策略上均取得了**最大闭环性能提升**
- 对于初始性能较弱的策略（如MindDrive和SparseDriveV2），提升幅度更大
- 在安全关键行为（如VRU交互、障碍物避让、路口转弯）上提升最为显著
- 消融实验显示，完整CRAFT框架相比仅使用反事实代理（GRPO）或仅使用闭环RL（PPO）均有明显优势

qa_pairs.question: 论文的主要局限性有哪些？未来可能的改进方向是什么？
qa_pairs.answer:
## 论文的主要局限性

- **对交互分布依赖性**：CRAFT 的改进效果部分依赖于用于微调的交互分布。在跨场景迁移实验中，Route Completion 指标的提升有限，表明在未见过的场景中策略趋于保守，泛化能力仍有提升空间。

- **反事实世界的固有偏差**：尽管 CRAFT 通过接地残差校正来缓解反事实代理的偏差，但反事实世界本身（非反应式模拟器、衰减滚动模型）无法完全捕捉真实闭环交互的动态特性，这种偏差在复杂交互场景中可能仍然存在。

- **计算开销**：CRAFT 需要同时运行反事实世界评估（对每个访问状态的候选轨迹进行模拟）和真实闭环交互，这带来了额外的计算成本。虽然论文提到通过异步收集和训练管线提高了效率，但整体训练时间（6-10小时）仍相对较长。

- **对策略架构的依赖**：CRAFT 在具有更宽动作空间的策略（如 HiP-AD 和 SparseDriveV2）上表现更好，而在动作空间受限的策略（如 MindDrive）上改进较小，表明方法的有效性部分依赖于策略的候选轨迹多样性。

- **超参数敏感性**：CRAFT 涉及多个损失权重（λ_cp、λ_gr、β_r、β_f）、裁剪参数（ϵ_clip、c）和折扣因子（γ、γ_c），这些超参数需要针对不同策略和场景进行调整，增加了实际部署的复杂性。

## 未来可能的改进方向

- **改进反事实世界的保真度**：
  - 引入更真实的交通参与者模型（如基于学习的交互模型）替代当前的衰减滚动模型
  - 使用世界模型或生成式模型来生成更逼真的反事实未来状态
  - 增加反事实评估的虚拟时间范围，以捕获更长程的交互影响

- **增强跨场景泛化能力**：
  - 在更广泛、更多样化的场景分布上进行微调，减少对特定交互分布的过拟合
  - 引入域自适应技术，使策略在未见过的场景中保持鲁棒性
  - 探索多任务学习框架，同时优化多个场景类型的性能

- **降低计算开销**：
  - 优化反事实世界的模拟效率，例如通过并行化或近似方法
  - 设计自适应采样策略，仅在关键状态进行密集的反事实评估
  - 探索更轻量级的反事实代理，减少对完整模拟的依赖

- **扩展至更广泛的策略架构**：
  - 研究如何将 CRAFT 应用于连续动作空间的策略（如扩散模型）
  - 探索在端到端感知-规划联合模型上的应用，而不仅仅是规划模块
  - 将框架扩展到多模态决策场景（如变道、超车等复杂决策）

- **理论深化与自动化**：
  - 开发自适应超参数调整机制，减少手动调参需求
  - 理论上分析代理-残差分解的最优权重分配
  - 探索自动化的反事实世界设计，使其能够根据策略表现动态调整评估标准

- **安全性与可解释性**：
  - 在安全关键场景中引入形式化验证，确保策略改进不会引入新的安全风险
  - 开发可解释性工具，帮助理解反事实代理和接地残差各自贡献的改进
  - 建立策略行为的保证边界，特别是在分布外场景中的行为约束
